ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry specific to Myanmar sweet vermicelli noodles; closest parent 'rice noodle food product' (FOODON_03311069) used as anchor.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is approximately 250 g, representing a standard bowl of Myanmar sweet vermicelli noodles as served at a restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Rice vermicelli, cooked — FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table; sugar and sesame oil component adjustment via USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Myanmar sweet vermicelli noodles (သကြား မြန်မာဒိန်ချည်) exists in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA databases. Values estimated from cooked rice vermicelli base (FAO/INFOODS SEA) with added sugar (~8 g per 100 g dish) and sesame oil (~3 g per 100 g dish) proportional adjustment. This reflects a typical restaurant preparation as served at Golden Cart Myanmar Restaurant. Macros recalculated accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice vermicelli, cooked (GI ~53)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Myanmar sweet vermicelli noodles. GI of 53 is taken from cooked rice vermicelli as listed in the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI and GL Values (Atkinson et al., 2021, Am J Clin Nutr). The added sugar component (~8 g per 100 g) modestly elevates effective glycemic response; GI retained at 53 as a conservative estimate. GL per 250 g serving = (53 × 34.5 × 2.5) / 100 ≈ 46.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~120 mg per 100 g; ~300 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be mindful of sodium intake from this dish, particularly if additional sauces or condiments are added at the table; a single bowl contributes approximately 300 mg sodium, which is 13% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load with added sugar (~8 g per 100 g) and moderate GI of 53
type2_diabetes.guidance: Persons with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size of this dish due to its high refined carbohydrate content and added sugar, which can elevate postprandial blood glucose; pairing with a protein or fibre source may help blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.4 g per 100 g) and cholesterol-free formulation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Myanmar sweet vermicelli noodles are low in saturated fat and contain no cholesterol, making them a relatively heart-friendly carbohydrate option when consumed in moderate portions and without high-fat accompaniments.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Myanmar Sweet Vermicelli Noodles (သကြား မြန်မာဒိန်ချည်, romanized: Thagya Mont Kyaw or Shan Khao Swè) is a traditional Burmese noodle dish made from thin rice vermicelli dressed with sugar, sesame oil, and sesame seeds. The dish originates from Myanmar and is popular as a light meal or snack. The noodles are first boiled until tender, then tossed with granulated sugar and sesame oil, giving them a subtly sweet, nutty flavour with a soft, slightly chewy texture. The dish is naturally gluten-free and vegan, making it suitable for a range of dietary preferences. In Singapore, it is served at Burmese restaurants such as Golden Cart Myanmar Restaurant. Nutritionally, the dish is predominantly carbohydrate-based (~34.5 g per 100 g), with modest protein (~2.8 g) and low fat (~2.5 g). The added sugar contributes to a moderate glycemic index of approximately 53 (low GI category), though the glycemic load per serving is significant (~46) due to the large portion size. Clinically, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size carefully. Sodium content is moderate (~120 mg per 100 g), relevant for those managing hypertension. The dish is low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, posing minimal cardiovascular risk when consumed in moderation.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Myanmar sweet vermicelli noodles (သကြား မြန်မာဒိန်ချည်) was found in any approved primary source (FAO/INFOODS SEA, USDA, HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN). Nutrient values are estimated from cooked rice vermicelli base data with proportional adjustments for added sugar and sesame oil. GI is analogously estimated from cooked rice vermicelli in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore low.
notes: 1. Food identity: The input food name 'သကြား မြန်မာဒိန်ချည်' translates from Burmese as 'sugar Myanmar thin noodles' or 'sweet Myanmar vermicelli'. The cuisine tags include 'Lor Mee', which is a Singaporean/Malaysian dish; this tag appears to be a field survey platform misclassification and has been disregarded. The dish is treated as a Burmese sweet rice vermicelli preparation. 2. Nutrient estimation: No direct database entry exists. Values estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA cooked rice vermicelli base (~150 kcal, 2.5 g protein, 0.3 g fat, 33 g carbohydrate per 100 g) with added sugar (~8 g per 100 g, +32 kcal) and sesame oil (~3 g per 100 g, +27 kcal) adjustments, yielding ~175 kcal per 100 g. 3. Sentinel values: vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA rice vermicelli entry. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the source. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 4. Multilingual naming: This is a Burmese dish, not a Singaporean dish; therefore the Singaporean multilingual requirement (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) does not apply. The romanized local name is provided. 5. GI: GL per serving = (53 × 34.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 45.7, rounded to 46.0. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as sesame oil and rice vermicelli contain no industrially produced trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a vegan dish with no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per FAO/INFOODS SEA rice vermicelli entry; no vitamin C-containing ingredients present. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based dish with no UV-exposed mushrooms or fortified ingredients.
